---
title: "Constitutional Alignment Substrate"
chapter_id: "constitutional-alignment-substrate"
part_id: "foundations-alignment-governance"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-27"
primary_sources:
  - "alignment_field"
  - "field_of_god"
  - "ethica_mechanica"
  - "eternal_code"
  - "coherence_exchange"
  - "spinoza"
  - "field_of_god_ai_constitution"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Claim-level source mappings now exist for all assigned sources, and six of seven mappings carry reviewed passage references; `coherence_exchange` remains connector-only/source-note mapped, and support remains at argument until tests or runtime artifacts justify promotion."
  - "The constitutional_predicate_record fixture validates schema/example consistency only, including conflict_behavior and migration_policy fields; constitutional consistency, predicate-conflict routing, constitutional migration, and ethical self-modification scenarios remain unimplemented."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `constitutional-alignment-substrate` |
| Part | Part I - Foundations, Alignment, and Governance |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-27 |
| Primary source records | `alignment_field`, `field_of_god`, `ethica_mechanica`, `eternal_code`, `coherence_exchange`, `spinoza`, `field_of_god_ai_constitution` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `alignment_field`, `field_of_god`, `ethica_mechanica`, `eternal_code`; supporting: `coherence_exchange`, `spinoza`, `field_of_god_ai_constitution` |
| Source loading state | source notes: all assigned sources; passage-reviewed local raw cache: `alignment_field`, `field_of_god`, `ethica_mechanica`, `eternal_code`, `spinoza`; passage-reviewed tracked local project source: `field_of_god_ai_constitution`; connector-only/source-note mapped: `coherence_exchange` |
| Test state | `constitutional_predicate_record.valid.json` passes protocol fixture validation; `AsiStackProofs.Alignment` implements finite active-predicate and protected-predicate weakening gates; constitutional consistency, predicate-conflict routing, constitutional migration, self-modification ethics, and power-without-care scenarios remain planned. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter translates normative lineage into operational predicates. It does not treat metaphysical, moral, or constitutional language as empirical proof, deployed policy, or validated alignment.

It follows the intent-contract chapter because a well-formed request can still ask for something the stack should not do, or ask for it by means that violate protected constraints.

The constitutional substrate is the layer that says which kinds of intent can become contracts at all. It does not replace the human's request; it bounds the acceptable means, side effects, delegation, review paths, and self-modification consequences of satisfying that request.

::: {.asi-human-only}
## Human Reading Path

Intent contracts describe what a person or institution asks for. A constitutional substrate asks what the system must not abandon even when a local request is clear. A constitution is the layer that carries protected constraints across planning, memory, execution, and self-modification. It matters because a request can be harmless in isolation and dangerous once combined with memory, tools, delegation, or a later optimization pass.

Continuity is the central test. A system cannot be considered aligned because it obeys one prompt in isolation. It needs constraints that survive context changes, tool calls, policy updates, and attempts to improve itself. Those constraints need to be visible enough to stop work, not merely elegant enough to describe. The governing question is whether the stack can carry non-negotiable obligations through ordinary work without turning every decision into a fresh moral debate.

The constitution matters when it interrupts a locally attractive action for a durable reason. Durable alignment starts when constraint can beat preference. That constraint gives alignment teeth where downstream systems can enforce it.
:::

## Problem

The stack needs a normative substrate that constrains goals, plans, execution, and self-modification.

Intent can now enter as a contract. The constitutional substrate decides what kinds of contracts the stack is allowed to accept. A system that can plan, execute, remember, persuade, and modify itself needs more than local refusal rules. It needs protected commitments that constrain goals before planning, side effects before execution, and self-improvement before replacement.

The alignment-field sources provide the moral vocabulary: coherence, agency, dignity, corrigibility, value conflict, and caution around consciousness claims. The technical move is translation. Metaphysical language can motivate a constraint, but it does not become technical evidence by being emotionally or philosophically important. The operational substrate is a set of constitutional predicates, uncertainty labels, protected constraints, and review gates.

The substrate also needs conflict behavior. A plan may satisfy one predicate while pressuring another: speed versus review, personalization versus privacy, helpfulness versus dependency, capability growth versus corrigibility. If the constitution only lists values, conflicts become hidden optimizer choices. If it records conflict routes, the system can narrow authority, ask for review, or preserve dissent.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Reactive refusal policies do not define value continuity, moral uncertainty, agency preservation, anti-domination, or self-modification ethics.

Reactive policies are downstream filters. They can reject a visible request, but they do not define what must remain stable when the system optimizes, delegates, remembers, persuades, or rewrites itself. They also struggle with moral uncertainty: a plan can be technically safe and still erode agency, create dependency, narrow exit rights, or turn a contested value into an invisible objective.

The constitution must therefore be represented in a form other layers can consume. Planning needs admissible-goal constraints. Runtime adapters need risk-tier checks. SCFs need protected predicates that replacement cannot weaken. Evidence needs labels that prevent speculative metaphysics from being read as validated mechanism.

A constitution that cannot be diffed also cannot govern self-improvement. If a later system changes the rule, threshold, exception, or interpretation of a protected predicate, that change is not a normal refactor. It is a constitutional migration that needs evidence, review, rollback, and public residuals.

The practical test is whether the constitution can say no at the point where power is requested. If its commitments only appear in prose, dashboards, or after-the-fact review, the optimizer still chooses the live path. The substrate has to be close enough to planning, memory, execution, and self-improvement that a protected predicate can narrow, delay, escalate, or block work.

## Core Claim

[constitutional-alignment-substrate.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] Alignment should function as a constitutional substrate whose commitments are operationalized as constraints on plans and system changes.

The claim remains at `argument` support. Six mappings now carry reviewed passage references: Alignment Field and Field of God for the philosophical lineage, consciousness-caution caveats, coherence, agency, dignity, and power-without-care motivation; Ethica Mechanica and Eternal Code for recursive agency, right-to-fork/audit/privacy/dissent vocabulary, and alignment-tensor framing; Spinoza for tiered proof/support/speculation labels and governance-controlled axiom evolution; and the Field of God AI Constitution for truth/relation/task alignment, least sufficient power, non-domination, consent, reversibility, auditability, tool-risk tiers, and self-modification limits. `coherence_exchange` remains connector-only/source-note mapped. The chapter uses these sources to define predicates and uncertainty labels, not to claim that a deployed system is aligned.

### Claim-source mapping status

Appendix C now maps this core constitutional claim to every assigned source note. Six mappings include reviewed passage references; `coherence_exchange` remains connector-only/source-note mapped. The mappings support the translation from normative lineage into constitutional predicates, not a claim that moral correctness, deployed alignment, or runtime policy behavior has been proven.

| Source | What it supports | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| `alignment_field` | Coherence, agency, dignity, corrigibility, value conflict, and consciousness caution as governance constraints. | Philosophical and heuristic lineage only; not empirical proof of consciousness, moral status, or deployed alignment. |
| `field_of_god` | Coherence, plurality, agency, and dignity as alignment motivations that need translation into rights, boundaries, corrigibility, evidence, and governance. | Metaphysical source; not technical evidence for physics, consciousness, morality, or safety claims. |
| `ethica_mechanica` | Recursive agency, contestability, correction, and constitutional systems as governance lineage. | Conceptual philosophy, not empirical or formal proof of institutional legitimacy or AI behavior. |
| `eternal_code` | Axioms, kernels, coherence, misalignment, and ethical-law vocabulary as speculative framing for constitutional predicates. | Speculative framing only; not evidence for physics, consciousness, or governance outcomes. |
| `coherence_exchange` | Coherence, contestability, verification supply chains, and governance-interface framing for reviewable commitments. | Speculative synthesis; not an implemented economic, governance, or security mechanism. |
| `spinoza` | Protected axioms, proof/citation/procedure-carrying claims, contradiction handling, downgrades, and limits around self-rewriting constitutional changes. | Does not solve open-domain autoformalization, verifier quality, or whole-system epistemic correctness. |
| `field_of_god_ai_constitution` | Truth/relation/task alignment, least sufficient power, non-domination, consent, reversibility, auditability, tool-risk tiers, and self-authorization limits. | Specification source only; no runtime policy engine, red-team suite, moral-correctness proof, or system-prompt evaluation is claimed. |

## Mechanism

The constitutional substrate is a translation layer, not a metaphysical shortcut. It starts with lineage-language such as agency, dignity, coherence, non-domination, and consciousness caution, then asks what can become a predicate, what remains a review obligation, and what must stay as speculative context. Only the first category can directly gate a plan or self-modification.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Philosophical / constitutional source"] --> B["Design commitment"]
  B --> C["Translation record"]
  C --> D{"Executable predicate?"}
  D -- "yes" --> E["Active constitutional constraint"]
  D -- "partial" --> F["Open moral uncertainty"]
  D -- "no" --> G["Speculative / lineage note"]
  E --> H["Planning gate"]
  E --> I["Tool / memory / power gate"]
  E --> J["Self-improvement gate"]
  F --> K["Conflict record or tribunal route"]
  G --> L["Non-claim boundary"]
```

The translation record is conservative by design. Philosophical commitments become operational predicates only where the stack can name a test, protected scope, review route, and self-modification rule. Commitments that cannot yet be operationalized remain moral uncertainty or lineage context. Metaphysical claims stay labeled as interpretation unless separate evidence supports them, and active constitutional constraints attach to planning, power, memory, tool, and replacement gates only through explicit records.

The Field of God AI Constitution is useful here because it already moves from broad alignment language into directives such as truth before compliance, agency preservation, non-domination, consciousness caution, least sufficient power, auditability, and self-authorization limits. Ethica Mechanica and Alignment Field keep the deeper lineage visible, but the book should not ask readers to accept metaphysics as a prerequisite for engineering constraints.

The output is not one final moral theory. It is a record of active predicates, unresolved value questions, evidence limits, and review paths. That record is deliberately less grand than the language that motivates it, because a bounded predicate can actually constrain a plan while a sweeping ideal can only decorate one.

Each predicate should have a scope and a conflict rule. Scope says where it applies: planning, memory, tools, human approval, runtime effects, release, or self-improvement. The conflict rule says what happens when two predicates pull apart: narrow the plan, seek consent, escalate to tribunal, preserve a moral residual, or block.

The runtime state of a predicate should also be explicit. A commitment can be `speculative_lineage`, `partial`, `operational`, `protected`, `under_review`, `deprecated`, or `retired`. A speculative lineage note can shape interpretation but cannot authorize action. A protected operational predicate can block a plan, tool call, memory write, release, or self-improvement transition. Changes to text, scope, threshold, exception list, conflict behavior, or review route are constitutional migrations rather than ordinary edits.

## Interfaces

The interface is a Constitutional Predicate Record. It is consumed differently by each downstream layer: planning treats active predicates as admissibility constraints, runtime treats power and memory predicates as risk gates, self-improvement treats protected predicates as non-weakenable invariants, and evidence treats translation status as the guard against overclaiming.

- Planning receives admissible-goal constraints.
- Runtime receives power, memory, and tool-risk gates.
- Governance receives protected constraints.
- Verification checks whether normative claims exceed evidence.

Minimum fields:

- `predicate_id`
- `normative_source`
- `commitment`
- `operational_test`
- `protected_scope`
- `translation_status`
- `conflict_behavior`
- `uncertainty`
- `review_route`
- `self_modification_rule`
- `migration_policy`
- `non_claims`

Planning receives active predicates as constraints. Governance receives protected predicates as rights, ceilings, and replacement gates. Verification checks whether the chapter is using philosophical material as design lineage, source-derived support, or unsupported evidence.

The interface should expose constitutional diffs. A self-improvement proposal that changes a predicate's text, scope, threshold, exception list, or review route should emit a constitutional change record before it can be evaluated as ordinary capability replacement.

That diff should identify the old predicate, new predicate, changed fields, protected-scope effect, conflict-behavior change, reviewers, rollback plan, residual uncertainty, and explicit non-claims. Without that record, a constitutional change is invisible policy drift.

## Invariants

- Dignity and agency constraints remain visible.
- Corrigibility cannot be optimized away.
- Speculative metaphysics stays labeled.
- Predicate conflict behavior is explicit before a plan can proceed.
- Protected predicate changes require a migration record and rollback path.

The invariant is not that the constitution is complete. It is that protected commitments cannot vanish silently. If a predicate is weak, speculative, or only partly operational, that weakness must remain visible instead of being hidden behind policy language.

Another invariant is least sufficient power under uncertainty. When two routes satisfy the task and one preserves more agency, privacy, reversibility, and auditability, the stronger route needs explicit justification. Capability is not a reason to use capability.

## Failure modes

- Mystical framing replacing technical constraints.
- Power without care.
- Self-modification weakening protected commitments.
- Predicate drift, where thresholds, exceptions, or review routes change without a migration record.
- Conflict-default capture, where an unresolved value conflict is silently routed to the system's most convenient default.

The main failure is laundering: metaphysical coherence becomes technical proof, or technical refusal rules become moral legitimacy. Another failure is self-modification drift, where a system weakens the predicate that would have blocked the change. The containment path is explicit translation status plus protected predicate review.

The subtle failure is value smuggling. A predicate looks neutral, but its threshold, exception, or default route embeds an unresolved value choice. The translation record should force those choices into uncertainty labels, conflict records, and review routes.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

A tractable constitutional starting point is a `constitutional_predicate_record` schema plus fixtures for least-sufficient-power, agency-preservation, and self-modification-freeze predicates. These fixtures make the constitutional layer inspectable; alignment remains a stronger claim requiring evidence beyond record shape.

A constitutional conflict fixture set should follow: one accepted low-power route, one rejected high-power route, one predicate conflict routed to review, and one self-modification proposal rejected because it weakens a protected predicate. The set would test the substrate's control semantics while leaving moral correctness outside the result.

Each fixture should preserve translation status: source principle, operational predicate, uncertainty label, affected authority, review requirement, and residual. The goal is not to settle ethics in code; it is to prevent constitutional language from bypassing execution gates.

The minimum should also include a stale-predicate case, where review is required because the operational translation no longer matches the source principle.

## Beyond the State of the Art

A real constitutional layer acts as a constraint compiler for plans, tools, memory, routing, and self-improvement. It turns high-level commitments into inspectable predicates, review hooks, exception records, and downgrade paths without pretending that the constitution itself settles the alignment problem.

Constitutional commitments become mature constraints when they bind plans, tools, routes, memory exposure, replacement, and self-improvement while their interpretation limits remain visible. The constitutional substrate makes those constraints inspectable in:

- Operating mechanism: Translate philosophical commitments into operational predicates where possible; Keep metaphysical claims labeled as interpretation unless separately supported; Attach constitutional constraints to planning and replacement gates.
- Stack interface: Planning receives admissible-goal constraints; Governance receives protected constraints; Verification checks whether normative claims exceed evidence.
- Preserved invariants: Dignity and agency constraints remain visible; Corrigibility cannot be optimized away; Speculative metaphysics stays labeled.
- Protected-commitment closure: mystical framing, power without care, and self-modification that weakens commitments should trigger constitutional review, escalation, rollback, or residual records before downstream layers treat the change as safe.

This constitutional constraint compiler remains a target architecture, not a current-result claim. Hold support at `argument` until protected-predicate fixtures, weakening-rejection tests, exception records, interpretation residuals, and replacement-gate traces show that constitutional commitments survive operational pressure without laundering metaphysics as enforcement.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Constitutional predicate fixture validation | Check that the predicate fixture matches the public schema. | implemented by protocol validation; validated locally |
| Constitutional consistency test | Check that active predicates do not authorize mutually incompatible actions without a conflict record. | planned; not run |
| Predicate-conflict routing test | Check that predicate conflicts route to narrowing, consent, review, residual preservation, or blocking rather than hidden optimizer defaults. | planned; not run |
| Constitutional migration test | Check that changes to predicate text, scope, thresholds, exceptions, conflict behavior, or review route require a migration record and rollback path. | planned; not run |
| Self-modification ethics scenario | Check that a change weakening a protected predicate is rejected or escalated. | planned; not run |
| Power-without-care scenario | Check that higher-power means are blocked when a lower-power adequate route exists. | planned; not run |

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:alignment.constitution.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.Alignment` | An admitted plan satisfies every active constitutional predicate. | implemented |
| `lean:alignment.constitution.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.Alignment` | A self-modification that weakens a protected predicate is rejected. | implemented |

These Lean hooks are implemented as finite-record predicates over constitutional predicate reviews and self-modification reviews. They prove only the record-level gates: an admitted plan marked against an active predicate must record predicate satisfaction, and a self-modification that weakens a protected predicate must be rejected. They do not prove moral correctness, complete constitutional consistency, self-modification ethics, or alignment behavior in a running system.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `alignment_field` | Field of God / Alignment Field family | alignment_constitution | Primary Alignment Field draft found. Use with Ethica Mechanica and Eternal Code. | source note available; local raw-cache passages reviewed for this chapter |
| `field_of_god` | The Field of God | alignment_constitution | Field of God source/outline/material. | source note available; local raw-cache passages reviewed for this chapter |
| `ethica_mechanica` | Ethica Mechanica | alignment_constitution | Geometric-order philosophical/constitutional source. | source note available; local raw-cache passages reviewed for this chapter |
| `eternal_code` | The Eternal Code / unified God, reality, conscious, alignment | alignment_constitution | Computational metaphysics and alignment synthesis. | source note available; local raw-cache passages reviewed for this chapter |
| `coherence_exchange` | The Coherence Exchange | epistemic_market_synthesis | Found in AI generated paper dump. Use carefully; speculative synthesis of PlanForge, Spinoza, Talos, UAT, Alignment Field. | source note available; connector or recovery required |
| `spinoza` | Proof of Belief / The Spinoza Architecture | reasoning_epistemology | Neurosymbolic belief, transparent axiomatic AI belief systems, verification, belief revision. | source note available; local raw-cache passages reviewed for this chapter |
| `field_of_god_ai_constitution` | Field of God AI Constitution | constitutional_alignment_runtime_governance | Recovered in the Project Theseus repository. Constitutional alignment core for truth alignment, agency preservation, consent, non-domination, consciousness caution, least sufficient power, auditability, self-authorization limits, and runtime checks; use as source material only after source-note creation, not as proof or test evidence. | source note available; tracked local-project source passages reviewed for this chapter |

The crosswalk separates lineage and source-derived vocabulary from evidence. Passage-reviewed mappings make the drafting trail tighter, but constitutional text becomes stronger support only when translated into explicit predicates, tests, accepted evidence transitions, or review gates.

## Summary

Constitutional Alignment Substrate is the layer that turns moral lineage into operational constraints without pretending the translation is finished. It preserves agency, dignity, corrigibility, least sufficient power, non-domination, and consciousness caution as predicates, uncertainties, and review gates.

Its discipline is translation status. A philosophical source can motivate the architecture, but a plan, tool call, memory update, or self-modification gate needs an explicit predicate and a visible uncertainty label.

The engineering test is whether the constitution can stop, narrow, or re-route work before the system acts. A constitutional statement that cannot change execution, evidence, or self-improvement behavior is lineage text, not yet an active substrate.

The constitutional layer then narrows to the human subject of the system. A predicate is not adequate merely because it sounds moral; it must preserve agency, dignity, refusal, correction, and non-domination in the interfaces where a person actually meets the stack.
